definition: Composite confidence score for a cross-exchange setup after execution friction, liquidity, stability and expected convergence are considered together.
interpretation: Higher values mean the model sees cleaner execution, better convergence support and less structural drag.
action: Use it as a prioritization filter, not as a standalone reason to force an entry.

definition: Estimated probability that the spread setup will converge closely enough to justify the trade after costs.
formula: model output based on spread anomaly, liquidity, funding drag and execution quality
interpretation: Read together with risk score and exit ETA. High probability with weak net return is still a weak trade.
action: Prefer setups where this stays high while expected net return remains positive.

definition: Estimated execution and outcome risk after slippage, thin depth, unstable structure and weak convergence support are accounted for.
interpretation: Lower is better. Rising values usually mean the edge can disappear before the trade closes.
action: Reduce size or skip trades when risk score rises faster than success probability.

definition: Adaptive execution cost estimate in basis points that reflects current bid-ask width, pressure on depth and route difficulty.
interpretation: This is the friction buffer the model subtracts before calling a trade attractive.
action: If dynamic cost is large relative to current net spread, wait for cleaner execution.

definition: Recommended delay before opening the position so the spread can stabilize or execution quality can improve.
formula: derived from confidence, spread velocity, execution difficulty and market regime
interpretation: Low values suggest the trade can be opened now; high values mean the model wants more confirmation.
action: Use it as a hard filter when you only want near-immediate opportunities.

definition: Recommended holding period for the setup before convergence is expected to lose most of its edge or funding drag becomes material.
formula: exit_eta_minutes / 60 with funding drag and regime adjustments
interpretation: Long holding horizons tie up capital and amplify funding uncertainty.
action: Prefer setups whose holding horizon still fits your capital rotation and operational window.

definition: Target normalized spread level or exit mode suggested by the AI layer for locking in gains before the edge is spent.
interpretation: The closer this is to zero, the more complete the expected normalization. Do not wait for perfection if the trade degrades first.
action: Pair it with current net spread and ETA so the exit plan is realistic before entry.

definition: Standardized distance of the current net spread from its own recent mean.
interpretation: Higher absolute values mean the current spread is statistically unusual versus recent history.
action: Use it to separate ordinary noise from genuine dislocation, then confirm with liquidity and regime.

definition: Percentile rank of the current spread compared with its own recent history.
interpretation: Values near 100 mean the market is near an extreme relative to its recent baseline.
action: Treat high percentile as context, then check whether execution friction still leaves real edge.

definition: Heuristic score that estimates whether large flows and volume impulses can destabilize the spread path.
formula: volume impulse + depth stress + CVD style flow adjustments
interpretation: Higher values mean larger flow-driven uncertainty and more unstable execution.
action: If whale activity is elevated, tighten size and demand faster convergence.

definition: Difference between funding rates on the two legs of the trade.
interpretation: Large divergence can either support the carry side of the trade or become a drag, depending on direction and holding time.
action: Always read it together with holding horizon and expected funding cost, not in isolation.
